meta.title: Saison 1 — Unités 0 & 1 : Mes cinq sens en action / S'ouvrir aux autres
meta.topic: Comprehensive A1 foundations from the Saison 1 textbook: greetings, alphabet, nationalities, colours, numbers 0–1000, emotions, days/months, francophone personalities, housing, rental vocabulary, physical descriptions, new modal and irregular verbs from class, and DELF A1 preparation.

vocabulary.notes: Feminine. One of the 4 monuments covered in Unité 0 video activity.
Masculine. The most visited museum in the world.

vocabulary.english: according to you / in your opinion
vocabulary.notes: Formal. Informal: 'd'après toi'. From class chat.
From class chat. Opposite: à gauche (to the left).
From class chat.
Adjective. Masculine: vrai. Feminine: vraie. Opposite: faux/fausse.
Masculine: faux. Feminine: fausse. Used in 'vrai ou faux ?' exercises.
Adverb. 'Je parle un peu français.' = I speak a little French.
Comparative adverb. 'Je voudrais plus de café.' = I would like more coffee.
Preposition. 'J'habite ici depuis 3 ans.' = I have lived here for 3 years.
Adverb. Also used to introduce a last point in a list.
Impersonal expression. 'Il s'agit d'un appartement meublé.' = It is about a furnished apartment.
Preposition. 'Grâce à toi, je parle français.' = Thanks to you, I speak French.
Physical description. Feminine adds -e: grande, petite.

vocabulary.french: il / elle a les cheveux châtains
vocabulary.notes: Brown hair = châtain (invariable). 'Il a les cheveux châtains.' = He has brown hair.

vocabulary.french: il / elle a les cheveux longs
vocabulary.notes: Hair description. Short: courts. Curly: bouclés. Straight: raides.

vocabulary.french: il / elle a les yeux bleus
vocabulary.notes: Eye colours: bleus (blue), verts (green), noirs (black), marrons (brown).
From class chat: 'Je suis une joueuse de squash.' Masculine: un joueur de squash.
Canadian French qualification — from class chat. DEC = Diplôme d'Études Collégiales.
Conjunction. 'Parce que j'ai beaucoup de travail.' = Because I have a lot of work. Before vowel: parce qu'.

verbs.notes: Irregular modal verb. Followed by infinitive: 'Je veux manger.' = I want to eat. KEY FORM: 'je voudrais' (conditional) = I would like — much more polite than 'je veux'. Always use 'je voudrais' in shops, restaurants, and polite requests.
examples: Je veux apprendre le français. — I want to learn French.
Je voudrais un café, s'il vous plaît. — I would like a coffee, please.
Elle veut visiter Paris. — She wants to visit Paris.

verbs.notes: Irregular modal verb. Followed by infinitive. 'Peux-je ?' is technically correct but rare — 'Est-ce que je peux ?' is standard. 'Vous pouvez m'aider ?' = Can you help me?
examples: Je peux parler français. — I can speak French.
Pouvez-vous répéter ? — Can you repeat?
Il ne peut pas venir. — He cannot come.

verbs.meaning: to live (to be alive / to experience life)
verbs.notes: Irregular. Note: 'vivre' = to live (be alive/experience). 'habiter' = to live (reside somewhere). 'Je vis à Paris' (I live in Paris, implying lifestyle) vs 'J'habite à Paris' (I reside in Paris). Also: 'la vie' = life (feminine noun).
examples: Je vis en France depuis 3 ans. — I have been living in France for 3 years.
Il vit dans un appartement. — He lives in an apartment.
Vivre en colocation, c'est pratique. — Living in shared housing is practical.

verbs.notes: Irregular. Note nous/vous forms: voyons/voyez (stem changes to voy-). Common in classroom: 'Regardez et dites ce que vous voyez.' = Look and say what you see.
examples: Qu'est-ce que vous voyez ? — What do you see?
Je vois des maisons et des appartements. — I see houses and apartments.
On se voit demain ? — Shall we see each other tomorrow?

verbs.notes: Irregular — conjugated like 'venir'. Note double-n in ils/elles deviennent. Common pattern: 'Il devient célèbre.' = He is becoming famous.
examples: Elle devient professeure. — She is becoming a teacher.
Je veux devenir médecin. — I want to become a doctor.
Il devient de plus en plus célèbre. — He is becoming more and more famous.

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb. Two meanings: 1) to win (a game, prize) 2) to earn (money). Context determines which. 'Il gagne le match.' = He wins the match. 'Elle gagne 3000 euros par mois.' = She earns €3000/month.
examples: Il gagne le match. — He wins the match.
Elle gagne bien sa vie. — She earns a good living.
Stromae gagne un NRJ Music Award. — Stromae wins an NRJ Music Award.

verbs.notes: Regular -RE verb. Note: 'entendre' = to hear (passive). 'écouter' = to listen (active). 'J'entends de la musique.' (I hear music — it reaches my ears). 'J'écoute de la musique.' (I am listening to music — deliberately).
examples: Vous entendez les sons ? — Do you hear the sounds?
On entend ses chansons à la radio. — His songs can be heard on the radio.
Je n'entends pas bien. — I don't hear well.

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb with spelling change: nous partageons (add -e- before -ons to keep the soft g sound). 'Partager son lieu de vie' = to share one's living space (Unité 2 title).
examples: Je partage un appartement avec deux amis. — I share an apartment with two friends.
Nous partageons les factures. — We share the bills.
Elle partage sa vie avec lui. — She shares her life with him.

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb. 'Chercher un logement' = to look for accommodation. Note: always followed directly by noun, no preposition needed (unlike English 'look FOR').
examples: Je cherche un appartement à Paris. — I am looking for an apartment in Paris.
Il cherche du travail. — He is looking for work.
Chercher quelqu'un qui... — To find someone who...

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb. 'Rêver de' + infinitive = to dream of doing something. 'À quoi rêvent les Français ?' = What do the French dream of? (Unité 2 section title).
examples: Les Français rêvent d'acheter une maison. — The French dream of buying a house.
Je rêve de voyager. — I dream of travelling.
À quoi rêvez-vous ? — What do you dream of?

verbs.notes: Irregular -IR verb that conjugates like an -ER verb. The double-l in singular/ils forms. 'Le phare accueille les clients.' = The lighthouse welcomes guests.
examples: Le phare accueille les clients le week-end. — The lighthouse welcomes guests at the weekend.
Elle accueille ses invités avec plaisir. — She welcomes her guests with pleasure.
On vous accueille chaleureusement. — We welcome you warmly.

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb. Always followed by 'à' — never drop it. 'Je ressemble à ma mère.' = I look like my mother. Used for physical resemblance.
examples: Je ressemble à ma mère. — I look like my mother.
Elle ressemble à son frère. — She looks like her brother.
Il ressemble à un acteur célèbre. — He looks like a famous actor.

verbs.notes: -ER verb with stem change: è (grave accent) in je/tu/il/ils forms. Used for weight. 'Il pèse 80 kilos.' = He weighs 80 kilos.
examples: Il pèse 10 kilos. — He weighs 10 kilos.
Combien pèse-t-il ? — How much does he weigh?
Ce sac pèse lourd. — This bag is heavy.

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb. 'Dessinez l'expression.' = Draw the expression (classroom instruction). 'Un dessinateur/une dessinatrice' = a cartoonist/designer.
examples: Dessinez l'expression idiomatique. — Draw the idiomatic expression.
Il dessine très bien. — He draws very well.
Je dessine un plan de mon appartement. — I draw a floor plan of my apartment.

verbs.notes: Irregular -RE verb, conjugated like 'écrire'. 'Décrivez votre logement.' = Describe your accommodation (textbook instruction).
examples: Décrivez votre maison. — Describe your house.
Je décris mon voisin. — I describe my neighbour.
Comment décrivez-vous cette personne ? — How would you describe this person?

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb. 'Demander à quelqu'un de faire quelque chose' = to ask someone to do something. 'Demander poliment' = to ask politely (Unité 1 speaking workshop section).
examples: Je demande de l'aide. — I ask for help.
Demandez poliment ! — Ask politely!
Il demande un café. — He asks for a coffee.

verbs.meaning: to find out / to inquire / to get information
verbs.notes: Both are reflexive (-ER) verbs meaning essentially the same thing. 'Je m'informe sur les cours.' = I get information about the courses. 'Elle se renseigne à la réception.' = She enquires at reception.
examples: Je m'informe sur les cours de français. — I get information about the French classes.
Elle se renseigne à la réception. — She enquires at reception.
Vous pouvez vous informer en ligne. — You can find out online.

grammar.title: Le genre et le nombre des noms (Gender and number of nouns)
grammar.explanation: Every French noun is either masculine (m) or feminine (f). There are no rules that work 100% of the time, but useful patterns: nouns ending in -tion, -sion, -ure, -ette, -ence are usually feminine. Nouns ending in -ment, -age, -isme are usually masculine. Plural: most nouns add -s (un appartement → des appartements). Special cases: nouns ending in -eau/-au add -x (un château → des châteaux, un bureau → des bureaux). Nouns ending in -s, -x, -z don't change (un bras → des bras). Nouns ending in -al often change to -aux (un journal → des journaux).

grammar.title: Le genre et le nombre des adjectifs (Gender and number of adjectives)
grammar.explanation: Adjectives agree with the noun they describe. Basic rule — add -e for feminine (grand → grande, petit → petite, lumineux → lumineuse). Add -s for masculine plural (grand → grands). Add -es for feminine plural (grande → grandes). Invariable adjectives (same for all): magnifique, pratique, célèbre, rouge, orange, marron. Irregular feminines: breton → bretonne (double n), rond → ronde, bon → bonne. Watch: adjectives ending in -eux change to -euse (lumineux → lumineuse). Adjectives ending in -ien change to -ienne (canadien → canadienne).

grammar.title: Les articles définis — le, la, l', les (Definite articles)
grammar.explanation: Used when referring to something specific or general categories. le (masc. sing.), la (fem. sing.), l' (before vowel or silent h), les (plural). From Unité 0 grammar box: Le → La → les. Used with emotions: 'La joie, la peur, la tristesse.' With days of the week in a recurring sense: 'Le lundi, je travaille.' = On Mondays, I work. Contrast with indefinite articles: 'J'ai un chat.' (I have a cat) vs 'Le chat est sur la table.' (The cat is on the table).

grammar.title: Oui, Non, Si — three ways to say yes/no
grammar.explanation: Oui = yes (affirmative answer to positive question). Non = no. SI = yes — used specifically to contradict a NEGATIVE question or statement. This is unique to French. Example: 'Tu n'es pas français ?' (You're not French?) — 'Si, je suis français.' (Yes I am French.) If you say 'Oui' to a negative question, it sounds like you're agreeing that the statement is true. Always use 'Si' to contradict a negative. From the Saison grammar box: 'Parfois à l'oral, on n'utilise pas le ne.' = In speech, the ne is sometimes dropped: 'Je sais pas.' instead of 'Je ne sais pas.'

grammar.title: Les pronoms sujets et toniques (Subject and tonic pronouns)
grammar.explanation: Subject pronouns: je, tu, il/elle/on, nous, vous, ils/elles — used before verbs. Tonic (stress) pronouns: moi, toi, lui/elle, nous, vous, eux/elles — used for emphasis, after prepositions, after 'et', and after 'c'est'. Examples: 'Moi, je suis français.' (Me, I am French — emphasis). 'C'est lui.' (It's him). 'Je parle et toi, tu écoutes.' (I speak and you listen). After prepositions: 'C'est pour moi.' (It's for me). 'Il vit avec elle.' (He lives with her). Note: 'on' can replace 'nous' in informal speech: 'On va manger ?' = Shall we eat?

grammar.title: La négation (1) — ne… pas
grammar.explanation: To make a sentence negative: ne before the verb + pas after. 'Je parle.' → 'Je ne parle pas.' Before vowel: ne contracts to n': 'Je n'habite pas.' From the Saison grammar box: Ne goes before the verb, pas goes after. TWO PARTS ALWAYS. Informal spoken French often drops 'ne': 'Je sais pas.' / 'C'est pas vrai.' — acceptable in conversation but not in writing. IMPORTANT: When a negative answer to a NEGATIVE question, use 'Si' not 'Oui': 'Tu n'es pas étudiant ?' — 'Si, je suis étudiant.' (Yes, I am a student).

grammar.title: Les articles indéfinis — un, une, des (Indefinite articles)
grammar.explanation: Used for non-specific nouns. un (masc. sing.) = a/an. une (fem. sing.) = a/an. des (plural) = some/any. After negation, un/une/des become de/d': 'J'ai un sac.' → 'Je n'ai pas de sac.' 'Il y a des livres.' → 'Il n'y a pas de livres.' Exception: after être, keep the article: 'C'est un médecin.' → 'Ce n'est pas un médecin.' (not 'Ce n'est pas de médecin'). From textbook: 'Un agenda (masc.), une boîte (fem.), des clés (pl.)'.

grammar.title: Les prépositions de lieu avec les villes et pays
grammar.explanation: With CITIES: always à (à Paris, à Calgary, à Bruxelles). With COUNTRIES: en + feminine/vowel-starting (en France, en Inde, en Italie). au + masculine (au Canada, au Japon, au Maroc). aux + plural (aux États-Unis, aux Pays-Bas). To say FROM: venir de + l' (de l'Inde), venir du (du Canada), venir de (de France), venir des (des États-Unis). From Saison textbook examples: 'Vous habitez à Bruxelles. Vous travaillez au Canada. Vous venez du Mali. Vous venez de Suisse.'

grammar.title: Consonnes finales muettes et e muet (Silent final consonants and silent e)
grammar.explanation: From the Saison Phonétique section (p.28): Final consonants are generally NOT pronounced in French. 'Une actrice anglaise' — the -e at the end IS pronounced because it follows a consonant. 'Il est libanais' — final -s is silent. EXCEPTION: final -r in certain words is pronounced (un chanteur). This affects nationality adjectives: 'français' (m) — final -s silent. 'française' (f) — final -e makes the preceding consonant audible. So masculine and feminine are pronounced differently! 'américain' vs 'américaine' — the final -n is pronounced in feminine.

grammar.title: Les questions (1) — est-ce que, qui est-ce, quel
grammar.explanation: Question forms from Unité 1: 'Qui est-ce ?' = Who is it? 'C'est qui ?' = Who is it? (informal). 'Est-ce que' turns any statement into a question without changing word order. 'Vous habitez à Paris ?' → 'Est-ce que vous habitez à Paris ?' 'Quel/Quelle' (what/which) agrees with the noun: 'Quel est ton nom ?' (m) / 'Quelle est ta nationalité ?' (f). Asking about 3rd person name: 'Comment s'appelle-t-il/elle ?' — the -t- is a phonetic liaison, not grammatical. Communication: 'Vous vous appelez comment ?' / 'Tu t'appelles comment ?'

grammar.title: Les verbes modaux — vouloir, pouvoir, devoir
grammar.explanation: Modal verbs express ability, obligation, or desire. All are followed by an infinitive. vouloir (to want): je veux → je voudrais (conditional, more polite). pouvoir (to be able to/can): je peux — 'Vous pouvez m'aider ?' (Can you help me?). devoir (must/to have to): je dois — 'Je dois partir.' (I must leave). Important: 'je voudrais' is the polite form of 'je veux' — in shops, restaurants, and any polite request, always prefer 'je voudrais'. 'Je veux un café' sounds rude. 'Je voudrais un café' is polite.

grammar.title: Habiter vs vivre — two ways to express 'to live'
grammar.explanation: Both can mean 'to live' but with nuance. 'Habiter' = to reside, to be located somewhere: 'J'habite à Calgary.' (I reside in Calgary). Followed directly by a place. 'Vivre' = to live in a broader sense — alive, experiencing life: 'Je vis en France depuis 3 ans.' (I have been living in France for 3 years — lifestyle). 'Vivre en colocation' = to live in shared housing (experience). In practice, both are often interchangeable for simple location statements, but 'vivre' implies more of a lifestyle element.

cultural_notes.title: La bise — French greeting customs
cultural_notes.content: In France, friends and family greet each other with 'la bise' — cheek kisses. The number varies by region: 1 in some areas, 2 in Paris, up to 4 in some regions. At work, a handshake ('se serrer la main') is standard, or simply 'Bonjour'. Between men, a handshake is most common. The bise is not done with strangers. When in doubt, follow the other person's lead. Cultural point from textbook: 'Généralement, on se fait la bise : une, deux, trois ou quatre selon les régions.'

cultural_notes.title: Tu ou Vous — a cultural distinction
cultural_notes.content: From the Saison Point culturel: 'On dit tu à quelqu'un que l'on connaît. On dit vous à un inconnu ou à son chef.' = Tu is for people you know, vous for strangers or your boss. This distinction is socially loaded in France — using tu with someone who expects vous can be seen as rude or presumptuous. Using vous is always the safe default with adults you've just met. In some contexts (young people, informal workplaces), tu is quickly adopted. The act of switching to tu is called 'se tutoyer'.

cultural_notes.title: Le français dans le monde — Francophone world
cultural_notes.content: From Saison Unité 1: 220 million people speak French in the world. French is spoken not just in France but across the Francophone world: Quebec (Canada), Belgium, Switzerland, Morocco, Senegal, Mali, Ivory Coast, and many other countries. The personalities studied — Kristin Scott Thomas (British), Mika (Lebanese), Akira Mizubayashi (Japanese) — all work in French despite not being born in France. Stromae (Belgium) sings in French and is heard on the radio in Mexico, Brazil, Russia and Canada. French is truly a world language.

cultural_notes.title: Fiches d'artistes francophones — class profiles
cultural_notes.content: Three key profiles from the Saison p.25 reading: Kristin Scott Thomas — born 1960, Royaume-Uni (UK), nationality anglaise, profession actrice, film 'Partir'. Akira Mizubayashi — born 1952, Japon, nationality japonaise, profession écrivain et professeur de français, book 'Une langue venue d'ailleurs'. Mika — born 1983, Beyrouth (Beirut), nationality libanaise, profession chanteur, song 'Elle me dit'. All three are non-French but work and create in French — illustrating the reach of the French language.

cultural_notes.title: La France en bref — key facts from Saison Actu Culture
cultural_notes.content: Population: 66.4 million. Area: 641,185 km². Capital: Paris. Currency: Euro. Official language: French. National holiday: 14 juillet (Bastille Day). Top 10 cities: Paris, Marseille, Lyon, Toulouse, Nice, Nantes, Strasbourg, Montpellier, Bordeaux, Rennes. Historical figures: Napoléon Bonaparte (emperor, 1769–1821), Charles De Gaulle (statesman, 1890–1970), Louis XIV (Roi Soleil, 1638–1715), Victor Hugo (poet and writer, 1802–1885). Top 6 influential French people (2013): Daft Punk, Christine Lagarde, Alain Ducasse, Delphine Arnault, Henri de Castries, Inès de la Fressange.

cultural_notes.title: Les jeux vidéo et l'animation 3D française
cultural_notes.content: France is the world's #1 cultural industry leader in video games and 3D animation. There are 250 companies in France in this sector. 'Moi, moche et méchant 2' (Despicable Me 2) generated $700 million worldwide and is partly a French production. French successes include Rayman Origins and contributions to Game of Thrones. American studios call on French expertise — for example, Les Schtroumpfs 2. The idiomatic expression 'Ça n'est pas ma tasse de thé' was taught in context: 'Les jeux vidéo, ça n'est pas ma tasse de thé. Je préfère lire des mangas.'

cultural_notes.title: Stromae — francophone music star (DELF reading)
cultural_notes.content: Stromae (real name Paul Van Haver) was born 12 March 1985 in Brussels. His mother is Flemish and his father is Rwandan. He has lived in the Brussels region since childhood. He started his career as a rapper in 2000, creating the rap group Suspicion. In 2009, he became a star with the song 'Alors on danse' and won an NRJ Music Award. He describes himself as 'un Belge qui a grandi avec la langue française et un peu de flamand.' His songs are heard on the radio in Mexico, Brazil, Russia and Canada. He is one of the most important francophone artists of his generation.

items.prompt: Je ___ un café, s'il vous plaît. (vouloir — polite form)

— Tu n'es pas canadien ? — ___, je suis canadien !
— Elle n'habite pas à Paris ? — ___, elle habite à Lyon.
— Tu ne veux pas de café ? — ___, je voudrais un café !
— Vous comprenez ? — ___, je ne comprends pas.
I would like to rent a furnished apartment.

extra_sections.label: 🎵 Audio Files — Saison 1 Exercise Map
extra_sections.content: 20 MP3 files uploaded for Saison 1. Each corresponds to a specific textbook exercise. Use these alongside the book pages.
items: Track 01 — Unité 0, Activité 2a, p.14 → Dialogues: Dans la rue / Au travail / À un concert (saluer)
Track 02 — Unité 0, Activité 2b, p.14 → Match dialogue to location/pronoun/phrase
Track 03 — Unité 0, Activité 3, p.15 → Jeux de lettres: Écoutez et écrivez les lettres

extra_sections.label: 🔢 Numbers 70–1000
extra_sections.content: The tricky numbers above 69 — new in this lesson from the Saison textbook p.30.
items: 70 = soixante-dix (literally: sixty-ten)
71 = soixante et onze
72 = soixante-douze ... 79 = soixante-dix-neuf
80 = quatre-vingts (literally: four-twenties) — NOTE: no 'et', and loses the -s when followed by another number
81 = quatre-vingt-un (no -s on vingt!)
84 = quatre-vingt-quatre
90 = quatre-vingt-dix
91 = quatre-vingt-onze ... 99 = quatre-vingt-dix-neuf
100 = cent
107 = cent sept
500 = cinq cents (adds -s when standalone, no -s when followed by number)
560 = cinq cent soixante (no -s because followed by number)
1 000 = mille (never takes -s)
TIP: Belgian and Swiss French use septante (70), huitante/octante (80), nonante (90) — much simpler!

extra_sections.label: 🏠 Rental Vocabulary — Quick Reference
extra_sections.content: Key vocabulary for understanding and writing housing ads (annonces immobilières).
items: louer — to rent | le loyer — the rent | les charges — utility fees
meublé / non meublé — furnished / unfurnished
un propriétaire — a landlord | un locataire — a tenant
une agence immobilière — a real estate agency
le rez-de-chaussée (RDC) — ground floor (= floor 0 in France)
le 1er étage — first floor above ground (= 2nd floor in UK/North America)
un ascenseur — a lift | sans ascenseur — no lift
un studio — a studio (1 main room) | un deux-pièces, trois-pièces etc.
meublé et lumineux — furnished and bright/sunny
disponible à partir du... — available from...
Typical rental question: Combien coûte le loyer ? — How much is the rent?
Typical rental question: Y a-t-il un balcon ? — Is there a balcony?
Typical rental answer: Le loyer est de 850 euros par mois, charges comprises.

extra_sections.label: 👤 Physical Description Vocabulary
extra_sections.content: From the Compréhension vocabulaire PPTX — describing people physically.
items: Il/Elle est grand(e) — tall | petit(e) — short
Il/Elle est blond(e) — blonde | brun(e) — dark-haired | roux/rousse — red-haired
Il a les cheveux châtains — he has brown hair (châtain = invariable)
Elle a les cheveux longs — long hair | courts — short | bouclés — curly | raides — straight
Il/Elle a les yeux bleus — blue eyes | verts — green | noirs — black | marrons — brown
Il pèse 80 kilos — he weighs 80 kilos (peser = to weigh)
Elle ressemble à Juliette — she looks like Juliette (ressembler à)
Describing style: Il est élégant. Elle est sportive. Il est sympa(thique).

extra_sections.label: 💬 Expressions idiomatiques — 5 new ones
extra_sections.content: Five new idiomatic expressions from the Compréhension vocabulaire PPTX.
items: Avoir le cœur sur la main — literally 'to have your heart on your hand' = to be generous
Avoir la tête dans les nuages — literally 'to have your head in the clouds' = to be daydreaming
Être dans de beaux draps — literally 'to be in beautiful sheets' = to be in trouble
Mettre la main à la pâte — literally 'to put your hand in the dough' = to roll up your sleeves / get involved
Donner un coup de main — literally 'to give a stroke of the hand' = to help out
Already known: Ce n'est pas ma tasse de thé — It's not my cup of tea = I don't like it
Already known: Ce n'est pas la mer à boire — It's not the sea to drink = It's not that hard

extra_sections.label: 🎭 Phonétique — Key Rules from Saison
extra_sections.content: Pronunciation rules from the Saison Point Étape (p.28) — tested in audio tracks 3, 7, 8, 15, 18, 20.
items: RULE 1 — Silent final consonants: Generally, the last consonant of a word is NOT pronounced.
français [frã-sɛ] — the -s is silent. libanais [li-ba-nɛ] — the -s is silent.
EXCEPTION: final -r is often pronounced: un chanteur [ʃɑ̃-tœʁ] — the -r IS pronounced.
RULE 2 — Silent final -e: The -e at the end of a word is generally not pronounced.
BUT: when -e follows a consonant, that consonant IS pronounced: anglaise [ɑ̃-glɛz] — the -s is voiced.
This means masculine and feminine forms often sound different: américain [a-me-ri-kɛ̃] vs américaine [a-me-ri-kɛn].
RULE 3 — Un vs une: 'un' = tongue forward + mouth slightly open. 'une' = lips rounded.
RULE 4 — Syllabation: French stresses the LAST syllable of each rhythmic group. Oui, MER-ci.
French word groups: bcd gptvw (lips closed) | flmnrsz (lips open) | ijyx (lips very closed) | eoug (lips rounded, closed) | ahk (lips wide open).

extra_sections.label: 📝 DELF A1 Preparation — Formats
extra_sections.content: From Saison p.38–39. The DELF A1 exam has 4 parts.
items: PARTIE 1 — Compréhension de l'oral: Listen to a document twice (30 sec pause between). Answer questions. Example: émission radio about astronaut Julie Payette.
PARTIE 2 — Compréhension des écrits: Read an article and answer questions. Example: biography of Stromae.
PARTIE 3 — Production écrite: Complete a form (library registration, profile). Example: Bibliothèque André Malraux form (nom, prénom, date de naissance, nationalité, profession, etc.)
PARTIE 4 — Production orale: 3 exercises: Entretien (answer questions about yourself), Échange d'informations (ask questions using given words), Jeu de rôles (role play a scenario).
Sample oral questions: Comment vous appelez-vous ? / Quelle est votre nationalité ? / Où habitez-vous ? / Vous avez quel âge ?
TIP: For DELF, always use VOUS forms in the oral exam unless told otherwise.

extra_sections.label: 🌍 Francophone Personalities — Saison p.25
extra_sections.content: Three artists from the 'Fiches artistes' reading — used for nationality, profession, and birth year practice.
items: A — Kristin Scott Thomas: née en 1960, Royaume-Uni. Nationalité: anglaise. Profession: actrice. Film: Partir.
B — Akira Mizubayashi: né en 1952, Japon. Nationalité: japonaise. Profession: écrivain et professeur de français. Livre: Une langue venue d'ailleurs.
C — Mika: né en 1983, Beyrouth (Liban). Nationalité: libanaise. Profession: chanteur. Chanson: Elle me dit.
Key sentences: Ils ne sont pas nés en France. Ils n'habitent pas en France. Ils ne parlent pas français tous les jours. Mais ils chantent/écrivent/jouent en français.
Grammar in context: 'Elle est née en 1960' = She was born in 1960 (être + past participle, feminine adds -e)
Question practice: Quelle est la nationalité de Mika ? / Comment s'appelle l'actrice ? / Quand est née la femme ?
